summary: The Docker-based reproduction builds Soft Serve from source, starts the server, registers a low-privilege collaborator, and attempts to import a repository from an internal HTTP URL (http://127.0.0.1:18080). If the internal server receives a request, SSRF is confirmed.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the Soft Serve server to make an outbound HTTP connection to the internal dummy server at 127.0.0.1:18080 when the collaborator runs 'repo import ssrf-proof http://127.0.0.1:18080/'. The internal server should log the incoming request, proving the SSRF vector.
actual_observed_result: Not attempted in this session. The reproduction bundle is self-contained and can be executed by running ./reproduction/run.sh with Docker available on the host.
verification_method: A Python HTTP server listens on 127.0.0.1:18080 inside the container. When git clone attempts to contact this address, the server writes a signal file to /tmp/ssrf_signal. The verify.sh script checks for this signal file to confirm the SSRF. This distinguishes the SSRF from a failed import that never attempted the connection.
evidence_produced_by_run: The internal HTTP server would log the incoming request with client address and path.
The signal file /tmp/ssrf_signal would be created confirming the internal service was contacted.
The Soft Serve server logs would show the import attempt with the internal URL.
limitations_or_blockers: The reproduction was not executed in this session due to build time constraints. The bundle is complete and self-contained for future execution.
The git clone to the internal URL will fail with a protocol error since the dummy server doesn't implement the full git smart HTTP protocol, but the connection attempt itself proves the SSRF.
Building Go from source requires network access for module downloads on first run.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the main branch, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
